text: Fever in adults: if persistent more than 3 days, seek medical review. If very high (above 40°C), or accompanied by confusion, stiff neck, severe headache, difficulty breathing, or rash, seek emergency care immediately. For children under 5, fever above 38.5°C warrants prompt medical attention, especially if accompanied by poor feeding, lethargy, difficulty breathing, or seizures. Keep the patient hydrated. Do not give aspirin to children.

Chest pain guidance (clinical/CVD): sudden crushing or squeezing chest pain, shortness of breath, cold sweat, nausea, or pain radiating to the left arm, shoulder, jaw, or back may indicate a heart attack — call emergency services immediately. Women may also experience atypical symptoms such as back or jaw pain and unexplained fatigue. If pain is mild, positional, or pleuritic, seek clinic-level review. Do not drive yourself to the hospital if a heart attack is suspected.

Breathing difficulty: any severe difficulty breathing, inability to speak full sentences, blue lips or face (cyanosis), or use of accessory neck and chest muscles requires emergency services. Rapid breathing (more than 30 breaths/min in adults) is a red flag. Mild cough without breathing distress can be monitored at home with rest and hydration. Breathing difficulty with wheeze and history of asthma — use reliever inhaler and seek care if no improvement within 15 minutes.

Stroke red flags (clinical FAST criteria): sudden Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty — call emergency services immediately. Additional warning signs include sudden severe headache with no known cause, sudden vision changes in one or both eyes, sudden difficulty walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination, and sudden numbness of face, arm, or leg especially on one side. Time is critical — every minute without treatment results in further brain cell loss. Note the time symptoms started.

Severe bleeding: uncontrolled bleeding that soaks through dressings or does not stop after 10–15 minutes of direct firm pressure requires emergency help. Apply continuous direct pressure with a clean cloth; do not remove the cloth if soaked — add more on top. Elevate the injured limb if possible. Internal bleeding (bruising, distended abdomen, blood in urine or stool) also requires emergency care. For nosebleeds, tilt head forward and pinch the nose for 10–15 minutes; if it does not stop or follows a head injury, seek emergency care.

Heart attack (myocardial infarction): symptoms include central chest pain or pressure, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea/vomiting, light-headedness, and pale appearance. Call emergency services immediately. While waiting: have the person sit or lie in the most comfortable position, loosen tight clothing, and give aspirin (300–325 mg to chew, not swallow whole) if not allergic and no contraindications. Do not give food or drink. Start CPR if the person becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally. Risk factors include hypertension, diabetes, smoking, high cholesterol, obesity, and family history.

Hypertension (high blood pressure): defined as blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg or higher on two separate occasions (clinical 2025). Most people have no symptoms. Very high blood pressure (180/120 mmHg or higher) can cause severe headache, chest pain, dizziness, difficulty breathing, nausea, blurred vision, confusion, nosebleeds, or irregular heartbeat — seek care immediately. Regular monitoring is essential. Lifestyle measures include a low-salt diet (under 2g/day), regular physical activity, avoiding tobacco and excess alcohol, and maintaining a healthy weight. Medication may be required; never stop prescribed blood pressure medicines without medical advice.

Diabetes: symptoms of type 1 and type 2 diabetes include excessive thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision, fatigue, and unintentional weight loss. In type 2, symptoms may develop slowly over years. Seek urgent care for: very high blood sugar (hyperglycaemia) causing extreme thirst, confusion, fruity breath, deep rapid breathing, or loss of consciousness (diabetic ketoacidosis); or very low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) causing shakiness, sweating, confusion, or fainting — give sugar immediately if conscious. People with diabetes are at higher risk of heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, and foot ulcers. Preventive care includes regular blood glucose checks, eye exams, foot care, and blood pressure control.

Asthma: common symptoms include persistent cough (especially at night), wheezing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness. Triggers include dust, pollen, smoke, cold air, exercise, and respiratory infections. For an asthma attack: use a reliever (bronchodilator) inhaler such as salbutamol — 4–10 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for the first hour. If no improvement after reliever inhaler, or if the person cannot speak in full sentences, lips appear blue, or breathing is very fast and laboured, call emergency services immediately. People with asthma should have a written asthma action plan from their healthcare provider.

Sepsis: a life-threatening emergency in which the body has an extreme response to infection. Common signs in adults include fever or abnormally low temperature with shivering, rapid heart rate, fast or difficult breathing, confusion or altered mental status, clammy or sweaty skin, extreme pain or discomfort, low blood pressure, and low urine output. In children: fast breathing, convulsions, pale skin, lethargy, difficulty waking up, and cold skin. People at highest risk include the elderly, newborns, pregnant women, and those with weakened immune systems. Seek emergency care immediately — early treatment is essential. Do not delay if sepsis is suspected.

Meningitis: inflammation of the tissues surrounding the brain and spinal cord — a medical emergency. Classic triad: fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. Other symptoms include sensitivity to light (photophobia), nausea, vomiting, confusion, and seizures. A non-blanching (does not fade with pressure) skin rash may indicate life-threatening meningococcal sepsis — call emergency services immediately. In infants: unusual irritability, bulging fontanelle (soft spot), high-pitched cry, poor feeding, and difficulty waking. Meningitis can cause deafness, brain damage, limb amputations, and death. Do not wait for all symptoms to appear — seek emergency care at first suspicion.

Depression and mental health (clinical guidelines): depression involves persistent low mood, loss of interest or pleasure in activities, fatigue, poor concentration, feelings of worthlessness, hopelessness, disrupted sleep, and appetite or weight changes lasting at least two weeks. Depression is different from normal sadness. Effective treatments include psychological therapy (cognitive behavioural therapy, interpersonal therapy) and medication for moderate to severe cases. Seek medical help if symptoms persist. Anxiety disorders involve excessive fear, worry, and avoidance. If someone expresses thoughts of suicide or self-harm, take it seriously: listen without judgment, encourage them to seek help, contact a crisis line or emergency services. You are not alone — help is available.

Headache: most headaches are tension-type or migraine and are not dangerous. Seek emergency care immediately for: 'thunderclap' headache — sudden onset severe headache described as the 'worst headache of your life' (may indicate brain bleed); headache with fever, stiff neck, and rash (possible meningitis); headache after a head injury; headache with confusion, vision changes, weakness, or difficulty speaking (possible stroke); headache with very high blood pressure. Tension headaches respond to rest, hydration, and over-the-counter pain relief. Migraine with aura involves visual disturbances followed by severe headache — avoid known triggers and seek treatment options from a healthcare provider.

Abdominal (stomach) pain: seek emergency care for severe pain with sudden onset, rigidity of the abdomen, vomiting blood or passing blood in stool, pain after an injury, severe pain with fever, suspected appendicitis (pain moving from navel to lower right abdomen with nausea and fever), or suspected ectopic pregnancy (severe pain with missed period). Moderate pain with vomiting and diarrhoea may indicate gastroenteritis — stay hydrated with oral rehydration solution. Avoid solid food until vomiting settles. Prolonged abdominal pain lasting more than a few days warrants medical review. Do not take laxatives or painkillers that mask symptoms before seeing a doctor for acute pain.

Diarrhoea and dehydration (clinical guidelines): diarrhoea lasting more than 3 days, bloody diarrhoea, or diarrhoea with high fever requires medical attention. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, reduced urine output, dark urine, sunken eyes, and dizziness — particularly dangerous in children and elderly. Management: drink oral rehydration salts (ORS — clinical formula: 1 litre of clean water, 6 teaspoons of sugar, 0.5 teaspoon of salt) or ORS sachets frequently in small sips. Breastfed infants should continue breastfeeding. Seek urgent care if unable to keep fluids down, signs of severe dehydration appear, the patient is a child under 2 years, or there is bloody diarrhoea. Avoid antidiarrhoeals in children under 5.

Allergic reaction and anaphylaxis: mild allergic reactions include hives, itching, and sneezing — antihistamines may help. Anaphylaxis is a severe life-threatening allergic reaction requiring immediate emergency action. Signs: sudden difficulty breathing, wheezing or stridor, swelling of throat or tongue, severe drop in blood pressure, rapid weak pulse, dizziness or collapse, and widespread hives. Call emergency services immediately. Use an epinephrine (adrenaline) auto-injector (EpiPen) if available — inject into the outer mid-thigh. Lay the person flat with legs raised (unless breathing is difficult, then sit them up). A second dose may be needed after 5–15 minutes. Hospital observation is required even if symptoms improve.

Burns (clinical guidelines): for minor burns — cool the burn under cool (not cold or icy) running water for 20 minutes; do not apply butter, toothpaste, or ice. Cover loosely with a clean non-fluffy dressing. Seek medical care for burns: larger than the palm of the hand; on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or joints; that are deep (white, brown, or black, painless); caused by chemicals or electricity; or in children and the elderly. For chemical burns, remove contaminated clothing and flush with water for 20 minutes. For electrical burns, do not touch the person until the power source is off. Burns affecting the airway (singed eyebrows, breathing difficulty, smoke inhalation) require immediate emergency care.

Poisoning and overdose: call emergency services immediately. Do NOT induce vomiting unless instructed by medical personnel — it can cause further harm. Keep the person calm and, if unconscious but breathing, place in the recovery position (on their side). If an opioid overdose is suspected (unconscious, slow or stopped breathing, blue lips, pinpoint pupils), administer naloxone if available and call emergency services. Provide as much information as possible about the substance: name, amount, and time of ingestion. For suspected ingestion of household chemicals, do not give food or drink. Common signs of poisoning include vomiting, confusion, seizures, difficulty breathing, chest pain, and unusual drowsiness.

Pregnancy complications: seek emergency care immediately for: heavy vaginal bleeding at any stage of pregnancy; severe abdominal pain; severe headache with blurred vision and swelling of hands/face (signs of pre-eclampsia or eclampsia); seizures during pregnancy; sudden decrease in fetal movements after 28 weeks; gush of fluid from the vagina before 37 weeks (preterm rupture of membranes); high fever; or symptoms of ectopic pregnancy (one-sided abdominal pain, shoulder tip pain, vaginal bleeding with a positive pregnancy test). Pre-eclampsia is a major cause of maternal death — blood pressure above 140/90 in pregnancy requires urgent medical review. All pregnant women should receive antenatal care.

Paediatric emergencies: call emergency services immediately for any child with: difficulty breathing, severe retractions, grunting, or blue/pale lips; unresponsiveness or difficulty arousing; seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or first seizure; high fever (above 38°C in infants under 3 months; above 40°C in any child); severe dehydration (sunken eyes, dry mouth, no tears, no urination for 8+ hours); head injury with loss of consciousness, vomiting, or abnormal behaviour; suspected swallowing of a foreign object or toxic substance; or severe allergic reaction. For febrile seizures: place child on their side to prevent choking, do not restrain, time the seizure, and call emergency services.

Head injury: seek emergency care for any head injury with loss of consciousness (even briefly), confusion, repeated vomiting, seizures, severe headache, clear fluid from nose or ears, unequal pupils, weakness or numbness in limbs, or worsening symptoms over time. Do NOT remove any penetrating object. Stabilise the head and neck if spinal injury is suspected — do not move the person unless necessary. A concussion may present with headache, dizziness, memory problems, and sensitivity to light/noise — rest is essential and medical assessment is required before returning to sport or physical activity. Children and elderly are at higher risk of complications.

Eye emergencies: seek emergency care for: sudden loss or significant change in vision; eye injury or penetrating wound (do not rub or press the eye); chemical splash — immediately flush with clean water for 20 minutes; sudden onset of floaters, flashes of light, or a curtain-like shadow across vision (signs of retinal detachment); red painful eye with nausea and vomiting (possible acute glaucoma); or eye pain with fever and reduced vision. For a foreign body in the eye, try flushing with clean water; do not attempt to remove embedded objects. Wear protective eyewear in hazardous environments. Sudden painless vision loss in one eye is a medical emergency.

Urinary tract infections (UTIs): symptoms include burning or pain when urinating, frequent urge to urinate, cloudy or foul-smelling urine, and pelvic pain. UTIs are more common in women. Seek medical care for diagnosis and antibiotic treatment. Seek urgent care if symptoms include high fever, back or flank pain, nausea or vomiting, or shaking chills — these may indicate a kidney infection (pyelonephritis), which is more serious. Drink plenty of water to help flush bacteria. Recurring UTIs require medical investigation. In men, UTIs are less common and always warrant medical review. UTIs in young children may present with only fever and irritability.

Fractures and musculoskeletal injuries: suspect a fracture if there is significant pain, swelling, bruising, deformity, or inability to bear weight or use the limb. Call emergency services for: open fractures (bone visible through skin); suspected spinal injury (do not move the person — stabilise head and neck); fractures with signs of poor circulation (pale, cold, numb limb); or pelvis and femur fractures (risk of severe internal bleeding). For suspected limb fractures, immobilise the limb in the position found using a splint, apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling, and seek medical care. Do not attempt to realign bones. Neck and back pain following an injury — keep the person still and call emergency services.

Heat and cold exposure: Heat stroke (core temperature above 40°C) is a medical emergency — signs include hot dry skin, confusion, rapid breathing, and loss of consciousness. Call emergency services, move the person to a cool place, and cool rapidly using cool water spray, wet towels, or ice packs on neck, armpits, and groin. Heat exhaustion (dizziness, heavy sweating, weakness, nausea) — move to a cool place, remove excess clothing, give cool water to drink, and rest. Hypothermia (abnormally low body temperature) — move to a warm environment, remove wet clothing, warm gradually with blankets, warm drinks if conscious. Frostbite — do not rub; rewarm in warm (not hot) water once back in a warm environment. Call for help if the person is confused or unconscious.

Drowning and near-drowning: call emergency services immediately. Remove the person from water safely — do not put yourself at risk. If unresponsive and not breathing normally, begin CPR immediately: 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths, continuing until emergency services arrive or the person recovers. All near-drowning victims require hospital assessment even if they appear to recover, as complications (secondary drowning) can develop hours later. Signs to watch for: coughing, chest pain, altered mental status, or difficulty breathing hours after the event. Prevention: supervise children near water at all times; learn to swim; use life jackets.

Snakebites and animal bites (clinical): for snakebite — keep the person calm and as still as possible, immobilise the bitten limb below heart level, remove jewellery and tight clothing near the bite, and transport urgently to a hospital that has antivenom. Do NOT cut the wound, apply a tourniquet, suck out venom, or apply ice. Identify the snake if safe to do so (without approaching it). For animal bites — wash thoroughly with soap and water for at least 15 minutes, then seek medical care immediately. Rabies is transmitted through saliva of infected animals — post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) with rabies vaccine must begin as soon as possible after a bite from a potentially rabid animal. Do not delay PEP.

Respiratory infections: common colds and mild upper respiratory infections can be managed at home with rest, fluids, and over-the-counter symptom relief. Seek medical care for: high or persistent fever; difficulty breathing or rapid breathing; coughing up blood; chest pain; confusion or altered mental status; or symptoms that worsen after initial improvement (may indicate secondary bacterial infection). Influenza (flu) may cause rapid onset fever, muscle aches, headache, and fatigue — antiviral medicines are most effective if started within 48 hours of onset. Annual flu vaccination is recommended especially for the elderly, pregnant women, young children, and those with chronic conditions. Pneumonia requires medical diagnosis and antibiotic or antiviral treatment.

Cardiovascular disease prevention (clinical): the most important modifiable risk factors are tobacco use, unhealthy diet (excess salt, saturated fats, sugar), physical inactivity, harmful alcohol use, and air pollution. Intermediate risk markers include high blood pressure, high blood glucose, and high blood lipids. clinical recommends: at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity per week; a diet rich in fruits and vegetables, low in salt (under 5g/day), saturated fats, and sugar; quitting smoking; limiting alcohol; and regular health checks (blood pressure, glucose, cholesterol). People with existing CVD, diabetes, or hypertension should be on appropriate medications as prescribed. Aspirin, statins, ACE inhibitors, and beta-blockers are core medicines.

Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disease affecting about 50 million people worldwide, characterised by recurrent seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Seizures can appear as convulsions with loss of consciousness (tonic-clonic), brief staring spells (absence seizures), or focal symptoms affecting one side of the body. During a seizure: do NOT restrain the person or put anything in their mouth; protect from injury by removing hard objects nearby and cushioning their head; time the duration of the seizure; and place in the recovery position after convulsions stop. Call emergency services if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus is a medical emergency); the person does not regain consciousness; there is a second seizure shortly after; the person is injured; or it is their first known seizure. Up to 70% of people with epilepsy can become seizure-free with appropriate antiseizure medication. Never abruptly stop epilepsy medicines without medical advice. Avoid known triggers including sleep deprivation, alcohol, and flashing lights.

Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects over 10% of the global population and is often asymptomatic in early stages. Symptoms in advanced CKD include fatigue, fluid retention and leg swelling, shortness of breath, nausea, itching, muscle cramps, and reduced urination. Acute kidney injury (AKI) is sudden kidney function loss and is potentially reversible if treated promptly; causes include severe dehydration, sepsis, and nephrotoxic medicines such as NSAIDs and certain antibiotics. Seek medical evaluation for persistent swelling, foamy or bloody urine, difficulty urinating, or if you have diabetes or hypertension (major causes of CKD). An eGFR (estimated glomerular filtration rate) below 60 for 3 or more months confirms CKD. Advanced CKD may require dialysis or kidney transplant. Prevention: control blood pressure and blood sugar; avoid overuse of painkillers (NSAIDs); stay well hydrated; and have regular kidney function tests if you have diabetes, hypertension, or a family history of kidney disease.

Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted through the bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. Symptoms appear 10-15 days after a bite and include fever, chills, headache, muscle aches, nausea, and vomiting. Severe malaria caused by P. falciparum is a medical emergency presenting with extreme fatigue, impaired consciousness, seizures, difficulty breathing, dark urine, jaundice, and abnormal bleeding. Seek emergency care immediately for severe symptoms. Diagnosis is confirmed by rapid diagnostic test or blood smear. First-line treatment for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria is artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT); severe malaria requires intravenous artesunate in hospital. Prevention includes insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs), indoor residual spraying, DEET-based repellents, and chemoprophylaxis for travellers (consult a doctor before travel). Malaria vaccines RTS,S and R21 are now clinical-recommended for children in high-burden sub-Saharan Africa. Seek diagnosis promptly if fever develops within weeks of visiting a malaria-endemic area.

Tuberculosis (TB) is an airborne bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs. Symptoms include a persistent cough lasting 3 or more weeks (sometimes with blood), chest pain, weakness, fatigue, unexplained weight loss, fever, and night sweats. TB spreads through the air when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or spits. Seek medical evaluation for any persistent cough with fever, night sweats, and weight loss. TB requires 4-6 months of antibiotic treatment using rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol; never stop treatment early as this promotes drug resistance. Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) requires longer and more complex regimens. TB and HIV coinfection is particularly dangerous as TB is the leading cause of death among people living with HIV. BCG vaccine given at birth protects infants and young children from severe TB. All household contacts of TB patients should be screened and offered preventive therapy if eligible. Strict adherence to the complete treatment course is essential to cure the disease and prevent resistance.

Dengue is a viral illness spread by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, most common in tropical and subtropical climates, with an estimated 100-400 million infections per year. Symptoms appear 4-10 days after a bite and include high fever (40 degrees C), severe headache, pain behind the eyes, muscle and joint pains, nausea, vomiting, swollen glands, and rash lasting 2-7 days. Warning signs of severe dengue (usually appearing after the fever breaks, especially with prior dengue infection): severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, rapid breathing, bleeding gums or nose, blood in vomit or stool, extreme thirst, pale and cold skin, and restlessness; seek emergency hospital care immediately if these occur. There is no specific antiviral treatment for dengue. Use paracetamol (acetaminophen) for pain and fever. Never use ibuprofen or aspirin as they increase bleeding risk. Rest and drink plenty of fluids. Hospital admission is required for severe dengue. Prevention: wear long clothing, use DEET-based repellents, eliminate standing water where mosquitoes breed, and use mosquito nets when sleeping during the day.

Cholera is an acute diarrheal infection caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae, transmitted through food or water contaminated with faeces. Most people have mild or no symptoms; however, severe cholera causes profuse watery diarrhoea (described as rice-water stools) and vomiting that can cause fatal dehydration within hours. Signs of severe dehydration requiring immediate medical care: sunken eyes, dry mouth, extreme thirst, loss of skin elasticity, little or no urination, rapid weak pulse, and collapse. Treatment: oral rehydration solution (ORS) is the primary treatment for mild to moderate cases; mix 1 litre of clean water with 6 teaspoons of sugar and 0.5 teaspoon of salt (clinical formula). Severe cases require intravenous fluids and antibiotics in hospital. Mass antibiotic prophylaxis is NOT recommended. Prevention: safe water supply, proper sanitation and hygiene, thorough handwashing after using the toilet and before eating, and oral cholera vaccine in endemic areas. During an outbreak, cook food thoroughly, avoid raw shellfish, and use only safe water for drinking and food preparation.

HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) attacks the immune system, making the body susceptible to other infections and cancers. AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is the most advanced stage of HIV infection. HIV spreads through blood, breast milk, semen, and vaginal fluids; it is NOT transmitted through casual contact, kissing, hugging, or sharing food or water. Early symptoms (2-4 weeks post-infection): flu-like illness with fever, headache, sore throat, and rash. Later: swollen lymph nodes, weight loss, fever, diarrhoea, and cough. Without treatment, HIV can progress to AIDS with life-threatening infections including tuberculosis and cryptococcal meningitis. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) taken daily for life suppresses the virus, restores immune function, and prevents transmission (an undetectable viral load = untransmittable, known as U=U). PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) prevents HIV infection in HIV-negative people at high risk. PEP (post-exposure prophylaxis) must be started within 72 hours after potential exposure. Get tested: early diagnosis enables early treatment and prevents onward transmission. There is no cure, but with ART people with HIV can live long and healthy lives.

Viral hepatitis infects the liver and can cause acute and chronic disease. Hepatitis B spreads through blood, sexual fluids, and from mother to child at birth; 254 million people live with chronic hepatitis B globally. Symptoms of acute hepatitis B include jaundice (yellow skin and eyes), dark urine, extreme fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Chronic hepatitis B (most common when infected at birth or in early childhood) can progress silently to cirrhosis and liver cancer over decades. Treatment: chronic hepatitis B is treated with tenofovir or entecavir; hepatitis B vaccine given at birth plus 2-3 booster doses provides nearly 100% protection. Hepatitis C spreads mainly through blood contact (shared needles, unsafe medical procedures); highly effective curative oral treatments now exist. Hepatitis A and E spread through contaminated food and water causing acute illness; hepatitis A vaccine is available. All adults with risk factors should be tested for hepatitis B and C. Avoid sharing needles, razors, or any item that may contact blood. Monitor for signs of liver disease: persistent fatigue, abdominal swelling, easy bruising, jaundice, or confusion, which warrant urgent medical review.

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is a progressive, incurable lung condition causing restricted airflow and breathing difficulty, responsible for 3.5 million deaths globally per year. Main symptoms: chronic cough with or without phlegm, shortness of breath especially on exertion, and wheezing. COPD is caused primarily by tobacco smoking; other causes include indoor air pollution (biomass cooking fires), occupational dust and fumes, and frequent childhood respiratory infections. Diagnosis requires spirometry testing. COPD exacerbations (flare-ups) involve worsening breathlessness, increased cough, and more sputum; they may require additional bronchodilators, oral steroids, and antibiotics. Seek emergency care if breathing is severely laboured, lips turn blue, or oxygen levels are very low. Treatment: bronchodilator inhalers are the core treatment; long-acting bronchodilators and inhaled corticosteroids for advanced disease; pulmonary rehabilitation; and oxygen therapy for severe cases. Self-management: quit smoking (most important single action), get annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccines, avoid indoor and outdoor air pollutants, and stay physically active. COPD and asthma share symptoms but differ in cause and reversibility.

Opioids include prescription medicines such as morphine, codeine, tramadol, oxycodone, and fentanyl (used for pain management), as well as illicit heroin. Opioid overdose occurs when a dose overwhelms the brain circuits regulating breathing. Signs of opioid overdose: very small (pinpoint) pupils, unconsciousness or unresponsiveness, very slow or stopped breathing, blue lips or fingernails, and choking or gurgling sounds. Call emergency services immediately. Naloxone (Narcan) is an antidote that rapidly reverses opioid overdose; administer by nasal spray or injection into the outer thigh if available and repeat every 2-3 minutes if no response; the person still needs emergency medical care even after naloxone reversal because naloxone wears off in 30-90 minutes. Place the person in the recovery position while awaiting help. Combining opioids with alcohol or benzodiazepines greatly increases overdose risk. People returning from prison or completing detoxification face very high overdose risk due to reduced tolerance. Treatment for opioid dependence includes methadone and buprenorphine (opioid agonist therapy), which are highly effective. Access to naloxone should be widespread in communities where opioid use occurs.

Oral health emergencies include: dental abscess causing severe throbbing tooth pain, facial or jaw swelling, fever, and difficulty opening the mouth; seek urgent dental or medical care as infection can spread to the jaw, neck, and airway. If face or neck swelling causes difficulty breathing or swallowing, call emergency services immediately (this is a life-threatening airway emergency). Knocked-out permanent tooth (avulsion): handle by the crown only, do not scrub the root, rinse gently with water, reimplant if possible, or store in milk or the patient s own saliva and reach a dentist within 1 hour for best chance of saving the tooth. Post-extraction bleeding: bite on a clean gauze pad for 30-45 minutes; if bleeding persists, seek dental care. Toothache self-care: rinse with warm salty water, floss to remove trapped food, use paracetamol for pain; but always see a dentist as toothache does not resolve without treatment. Prevention: brush teeth twice daily with fluoride toothpaste (at least 1000 ppm), floss daily, limit sugary food and drinks, avoid tobacco, and attend regular dental check-ups. Oral infections during pregnancy must be treated promptly as untreated infection can harm mother and baby.

Falls are the second leading cause of unintentional injury deaths worldwide and are especially dangerous in adults over 60, accounting for the majority of fatal fall deaths. Risk factors include muscle weakness, poor balance and gait, visual impairment, certain medications causing dizziness (diuretics, sedatives, blood pressure medicines), cognitive impairment, home hazards such as slippery floors, loose rugs and poor lighting, and conditions including Parkinson s disease and osteoporosis. After a fall: if the person cannot get up safely, stay calm and warm, and call for help; do not attempt to stand if there is significant pain especially in the hip. Seek medical care after any fall with significant pain, suspected fracture, head injury, or loss of consciousness. Hip fracture is a common serious consequence presenting as severe hip or groin pain with inability to bear weight; requires emergency evaluation. Other fall-related injuries include wrist and vertebral fractures, and head trauma. Prevention: exercise programmes for balance and strength (Tai Chi, physiotherapy), home safety modifications (grab rails, improved lighting, removing trip hazards), medication review by a doctor, regular vision testing, and vitamin D supplementation for those deficient. Fall prevention programmes can reduce fall rates in older adults by 30-40%.

Typhoid fever is a life-threatening bacterial infection caused by Salmonella Typhi, spread through food or water contaminated with faeces of an infected person. Symptoms develop over several days including prolonged high fever (often rising in a stepwise pattern), fatigue, headache, nausea, abdominal pain, and constipation or diarrhoea; some patients develop a rose-coloured rash. Without treatment, serious complications can occur including intestinal perforation and haemorrhage, which are surgical emergencies. Treatment: antibiotics (fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin or azithromycin as first line; note increasing antibiotic resistance in many regions); complete the full prescribed antibiotic course. Even after recovery, some people carry the bacteria in their gut and can spread it; maintain strict handwashing especially after using the toilet and before handling food. Typhoid can be confirmed with a blood culture. Prevention: typhoid conjugate vaccine is recommended from 6 months of age in endemic areas and for travellers; ensure safe water and food (cook food thoroughly, avoid raw vegetables and peeled fruits in endemic areas); practice thorough handwashing. Around 9 million people get typhoid annually, causing approximately 110,000 deaths per year.

Rabies is a viral infection of the central nervous system that is almost always fatal once symptoms appear. The virus is transmitted through bites or scratches of infected animals; 99% of human rabies cases globally result from dog bites, predominantly in Asia and Africa. Incubation period is typically 2-3 months but can range from 1 week to 1 year. Early symptoms include fever, headache, and unusual tingling or burning at the wound site. Progressive symptoms include confusion, agitation, hallucinations, hydrophobia (fear of water), paralysis, and coma. ANY bite or scratch from a potentially rabid animal requires IMMEDIATE action: (1) wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for at least 15 minutes; (2) apply iodine-based antiseptic or 70% alcohol; (3) seek medical care IMMEDIATELY for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) consisting of rabies vaccine and, for severe exposures (Category III), rabies immunoglobulin injected into the wound. PEP is highly effective when started promptly before symptoms appear. Pre-exposure vaccination is recommended for high-risk occupations (veterinarians, wildlife workers) and travellers to endemic areas. Mass dog vaccination is the most effective strategy to eliminate human rabies. Approximately 59,000 people die from rabies each year, 40% of whom are children under 15.

Malnutrition encompasses undernutrition (wasting, stunting, underweight), micronutrient deficiencies, and overnutrition (overweight and obesity). Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children presents as visible severe wasting or bilateral pitting oedema (swelling of both feet) and is a medical emergency requiring urgent therapeutic feeding and medical management. Signs of malnutrition across age groups: unintentional weight loss, muscle wasting, extreme fatigue, poor wound healing, hair thinning, and frequent infections. Key micronutrient deficiencies: iron deficiency anaemia (fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath; treat with iron-rich foods and supplements); vitamin A deficiency (night blindness and increased infection susceptibility in children; treat with vitamin A capsules); iodine deficiency (goitre, hypothyroidism, impaired child brain development; prevent with iodised salt); zinc deficiency (poor growth and immune dysfunction in children). Malnutrition screening is important in patients with cancer, chronic disease, post-surgery, or in the elderly. Therapeutic feeding for SAM uses ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF). In adults, a BMI below 18.5 indicates underweight and warrants nutritional assessment. Prevention: exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months, timely complementary feeding, dietary diversity, and micronutrient supplementation for vulnerable groups.

Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) affect over 1 million people daily worldwide. Common curable STIs include chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and trichomoniasis (treated with antibiotics); viral STIs including herpes, HPV, HIV, and hepatitis B are not fully curable but are treatable. Many STIs cause no symptoms, making regular screening essential for sexually active people with new or multiple partners. Common symptoms when present: unusual discharge from the penis or vagina; genital sores or ulcers; burning or pain when urinating; pelvic pain in women; and generalised rash (secondary syphilis). Seek testing and treatment promptly; untreated STIs can cause infertility, ectopic pregnancy, increased HIV risk, chronic pelvic pain, and complications during pregnancy and delivery including congenital syphilis. Prevention: condoms used correctly and consistently offer strong protection against most STIs including HIV; HPV and hepatitis B vaccines are safe and highly effective; PrEP prevents HIV in high-risk individuals. Partner notification and treatment is essential to break the transmission chain. Antibiotic resistance in gonorrhoea is a growing global threat; follow current local treatment guidelines. Routine STI screening in pregnancy prevents serious complications in newborns.

Proper wound care prevents infection and promotes healing. First steps: stop bleeding with direct firm pressure for 10-15 minutes; gently clean the wound with clean running water and mild soap to remove all visible dirt; apply an antiseptic (such as iodine or chlorhexidine) if available; cover with a clean sterile bandage or dressing. Change dressings daily or when soiled, keeping the wound moist and protected. Signs of wound infection requiring medical attention: increasing redness, warmth, and swelling around the wound; pus or increasing discharge; red streaks spreading from the wound (lymphangitis indicating spreading infection); fever; and increasing pain after initial improvement. Deep wounds, puncture wounds, animal bites, lacerations requiring stitches, and wounds contaminated with soil or faeces require prompt medical assessment and may need antibiotics and tetanus immunisation. Tetanus: ensure vaccination is up to date (booster every 10 years); after any high-risk wound in a person not fully vaccinated, seek tetanus prophylaxis immediately. Infected wounds that are not treated can progress to cellulitis, abscess, or sepsis. Do not close heavily contaminated or bite wounds with tape or sutures before medical evaluation.

Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) can double or triple survival from cardiac arrest. If you find an unresponsive person: shout for help and call emergency services (or ask someone to call while you start CPR). Check for normal breathing by looking, listening, and feeling for no more than 10 seconds. If not breathing normally, begin chest compressions: place the heel of one hand on the centre of the chest (lower half of the breastbone), place the other hand on top, keep your arms straight, and compress firmly at least 5-6 cm deep at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute. Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions without removing your hands. If trained and willing, give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (30:2 ratio); tilt the head back, lift the chin, seal your mouth over theirs, and blow for 1 second until the chest rises. Continue until the person recovers normal breathing, professional help arrives, or an AED (automated external defibrillator) is available. For AED use: turn on, attach pads as illustrated, follow all voice prompts, ensure no one is touching the person before delivering a shock. In children: use 2 fingers for infants or one or two hands for older children; compress to approximately one-third of chest depth. Every minute without CPR reduces survival by approximately 10%.

Anxiety disorders are the most common mental health conditions worldwide, affecting hundreds of millions of people. A panic attack is a sudden intense episode of fear causing physical symptoms: racing or pounding heart, shortness of breath, chest tightness, sweating, trembling, dizziness, nausea, numbness or tingling, and a sense of impending doom or unreality. Panic attacks are not physically dangerous despite feeling terrifying; they typically peak within 10 minutes and resolve within 20-30 minutes. To help someone during a panic attack: stay calm and reassuring; encourage slow controlled breathing (breathe in for 4 seconds, hold for 2, breathe out slowly for 6 seconds); remind them the feelings will pass; and guide them to a quieter environment. Seek medical evaluation to rule out cardiac or other physical causes, especially at first occurrence. Generalised anxiety disorder involves persistent excessive worry about everyday events, causing significant impairment. Effective treatments include cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), mindfulness-based therapy, and medication such as SSRIs or SNRIs. Avoid self-medicating with alcohol or benzodiazepines. Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) following trauma also requires specialist assessment. Anxiety disorders are treatable; early professional help leads to better outcomes.

Acute alcohol poisoning occurs when blood alcohol levels become dangerously high, impairing brain function and breathing. Symptoms: confusion, vomiting, seizures, slow or irregular breathing (fewer than 8 breaths per minute), blue-tinged or pale skin, hypothermia, unconsciousness, and inability to be roused. Call emergency services immediately if someone is unconscious or cannot be woken, even if they appear to be sleeping. Do NOT leave them alone as aspiration of vomit is a leading cause of alcohol-related death. Place an unconscious but breathing person on their side in the recovery position to prevent choking. Do NOT give coffee, food, or try to walk them around; these do not speed up alcohol metabolism. Do NOT allow a severely intoxicated person to sleep in an unsupervised position on their back. Mixing alcohol with opioids, benzodiazepines, or other sedatives greatly increases overdose and death risk. Long-term harmful alcohol use causes cirrhosis, pancreatitis, cardiomyopathy, multiple cancers, peripheral neuropathy, and severe mental health disorders. clinical safe use guidance: no level of alcohol consumption is completely without risk; if drinking, limit to no more than 1-2 standard drinks per day; avoid alcohol entirely during pregnancy, when driving, or when taking sedating medications.

Food poisoning (foodborne illness) is caused by consuming food or water contaminated with bacteria such as Salmonella, Campylobacter, E. coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Listeria; viruses including norovirus and hepatitis A; or their toxins. Symptoms typically begin within hours to a few days of eating contaminated food and include nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (possibly bloody), abdominal cramps, and fever. Most cases resolve within 1-3 days with rest and hydration. Management: drink oral rehydration solution (ORS) frequently in small sips to prevent dehydration; avoid solid food until vomiting settles; then reintroduce bland foods gradually. Seek medical care if: diarrhoea is bloody; fever is above 39 degrees C; you cannot keep any fluids down; there are signs of dehydration (dry mouth, extreme thirst, dizziness, little or no urine); symptoms last more than 3 days; or the affected person is an infant, elderly, pregnant, or immunocompromised. Botulism from improperly home-canned foods causes descending paralysis and double vision and requires immediate emergency care. Prevention: cook meat, eggs, and shellfish thoroughly; refrigerate perishables within 2 hours; avoid cross-contamination between raw and cooked foods; wash hands before and after handling food; avoid raw dairy, undercooked shellfish, and unpasteurised juices.

Common skin emergencies and conditions: severe allergic reaction with urticaria (hives) presents as large red itchy welts; treat with antihistamines; if throat swelling or breathing difficulty develops, treat as anaphylaxis and call emergency services immediately. Cellulitis is a bacterial skin infection causing spreading redness, warmth, swelling, and pain following a break in skin; seek antibiotic treatment promptly; red streaks, fever, or rapidly spreading infection require emergency care as they indicate bacteraemia. Scabies is caused by skin mites and presents as intense itching especially at night, with a rash between fingers, wrists, and other skin folds; treat all household members simultaneously with topical permethrin cream or oral ivermectin; wash all bedding and clothing. Shingles (herpes zoster) causes a painful blistering rash in a dermatomal pattern; antiviral treatment within 72 hours of rash onset reduces severity and duration; shingles involving the eye requires urgent ophthalmic care. Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a rare, life-threatening drug reaction causing a painful spreading red or purple rash, blistering of skin and mucous membranes, and systemic illness; requires immediate hospital admission; identify and stop the causative drug. Necrotising fasciitis is a rapidly spreading deep soft tissue infection causing severe pain, swelling, and overlying skin discolouration; it is a surgical emergency requiring immediate debridement.

Common ear, nose, and throat conditions: Otitis media (middle ear infection) causes ear pain, fever, and muffled hearing, most commonly in children; mild cases in children over 2 years often resolve without antibiotics but severe or persistent cases require treatment. Otitis externa (swimmer s ear) causes outer ear canal pain worsened by pulling the ear; treat with prescribed ear drops and keep the ear dry. Foreign body in the ear: do not insert objects to remove it; see a doctor. Epistaxis (nosebleed): sit upright with head slightly forward and pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10-15 minutes without releasing; seek emergency care if bleeding persists beyond 30 minutes, follows significant head trauma, or is associated with blood-thinning medicines. Strep throat (streptococcal pharyngitis): presents as severe sore throat with fever, white patches on tonsils, and swollen lymph nodes; requires a throat swab for diagnosis and antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever; complete the full antibiotic course. Epiglottitis: sudden severe sore throat, drooling, difficulty swallowing, a muffled (hot-potato) voice, stridor, and the child sitting upright with neck extended is a life-threatening emergency; call emergency services and do NOT examine the throat as this can cause complete airway obstruction. Tonsillitis or peritonsillar abscess causing airway obstruction or inability to swallow also requires emergency treatment.

Measles is one of the most contagious human viruses, spreading through the air. Symptoms appear 10-14 days after exposure: high fever, runny nose, red watery eyes (conjunctivitis), cough, and distinctive Koplik spots (tiny white spots inside the mouth) followed by a red blotchy rash starting on the face and spreading to the body over 3-4 days. Complications are more severe in malnourished children, infants, and immunocompromised people and include pneumonia (leading cause of measles death), encephalitis, blindness, and death. Treatment is supportive: rest, fluids, fever control with paracetamol, and vitamin A supplementation which reduces mortality. Seek emergency care for high fever with seizures, difficulty breathing, or confusion. Two doses of the MMR (measles, mumps, rubella) vaccine provide lifelong protection; measles vaccination is the single most important preventive measure. Other clinical-recommended vaccine-preventable diseases: whooping cough (pertussis causes violent paroxysmal coughing in infants, dangerous in newborns), diphtheria (membrane in throat can cause airway obstruction), polio (can cause permanent paralysis), tetanus (causes muscle spasms and trismus), and meningococcal disease (causes bacterial meningitis). Ensure all children receive all recommended vaccinations on schedule; adults should check their vaccination status.

Jaundice, the yellowing of skin and whites of the eyes, results from excess bilirubin and is a symptom of liver, bile duct, or blood disorders requiring medical evaluation. Common causes include viral hepatitis, alcoholic liver disease, bile duct obstruction from gallstones or pancreatic cancer, and haemolytic anaemia. Neonatal jaundice (appearing in the first few days of life) is common; mild physiological jaundice resolves without treatment, but severe jaundice in newborns requires urgent phototherapy or exchange transfusion to prevent bilirubin-related brain damage (kernicterus). Seek urgent medical evaluation for jaundice at any age, especially accompanied by dark urine, pale or clay-coloured stools, severe itching, right upper abdominal pain, or fever. Liver cirrhosis results from chronic liver damage (from alcohol, viral hepatitis, or fatty liver disease) and presents as fatigue, jaundice, abdominal swelling from fluid accumulation (ascites), easy bruising, and spider-like blood vessels on the skin. Complications of cirrhosis requiring emergency care: vomiting blood or black tarry stools from oesophageal varices (ruptured dilated veins around the oesophagus); confusion and altered consciousness from hepatic encephalopathy; and spontaneous peritonitis. Alcohol cessation is the single most important intervention for alcoholic liver disease. Avoid taking medications including paracetamol in excess doses if you have liver disease.

Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot forming in a deep vein, most often in the leg. Symptoms include pain, tenderness, swelling, redness, and warmth in the affected limb; however, up to half of DVTs cause no symptoms. Pulmonary embolism (PE) occurs when a clot travels to the lungs and is a medical emergency. Signs of pulmonary embolism: sudden unexplained shortness of breath, sharp chest pain that worsens when breathing, rapid heart rate, coughing up blood, and fainting or near-fainting; call emergency services immediately. Risk factors for DVT and PE include prolonged immobility (long-haul air travel, hospitalisation, post-operative recovery), cancer and its treatment, oral contraceptives and hormone therapy, pregnancy and the postpartum period, hereditary clotting disorders, obesity, previous DVT or PE, and dehydration. Prevention during travel: move legs regularly by walking the aisle, perform calf-raise exercises in your seat, stay well hydrated, and consider graduated compression stockings for long flights if at risk. Treatment with anticoagulants (blood-thinning medicines such as heparin and warfarin or newer oral anticoagulants) is required for confirmed DVT or PE. Seek medical evaluation promptly for any unexplained, persistent leg swelling or pain, especially combined with risk factors.

Anaemia is defined as haemoglobin below 130 g/L in adult men and 120 g/L in non-pregnant adult women; it affects approximately 2 billion people worldwide. Common symptoms include fatigue and weakness, dizziness, headaches, pallor (pale skin, lips, gums, and inner lower eyelids), shortness of breath on exertion, rapid heartbeat, and cold hands and feet. Severe anaemia can cause heart failure and requires urgent medical evaluation. Common causes: iron deficiency anaemia (most common globally; caused by poor dietary intake, chronic blood loss from menstruation, ulcers, or hookworm, or poor absorption); vitamin B12 or folate deficiency; anaemia of chronic disease; sickle cell disease (hereditary haemolytic anaemia causing painful crises requiring urgent pain management and hydration); thalassaemia; and malaria-related haemolytic anaemia in endemic regions. Iron deficiency anaemia management: increase dietary iron (red meat, dark leafy greens, legumes, fortified cereals); take prescribed iron supplements; consume vitamin C alongside iron-rich foods to enhance absorption; avoid tea and coffee with iron-rich meals as tannins inhibit absorption. Seek medical evaluation for unexplained anaemia, blood in stool, or anaemia with systemic symptoms. Anaemia in pregnancy increases risk of maternal and neonatal complications; routine screening and supplementation from early pregnancy is essential. Never self-prescribe iron supplements without medical advice in children.

Back pain is one of the leading causes of disability worldwide. Most acute low back pain (lasting under 6 weeks) is mechanical and resolves with self-care: remain as active as possible since prolonged bed rest worsens outcomes; apply heat to the sore area; and use paracetamol or NSAIDs as needed for pain relief. Urgent red flag back pain symptoms requiring immediate medical evaluation include: back pain following trauma or fall; bowel or bladder dysfunction such as incontinence or inability to urinate (suggests cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring treatment within hours to prevent permanent paralysis); numbness or tingling in the saddle area (groin and inner thighs); unexplained weight loss; fever with back pain (possible spinal infection or abscess); pain in a person over 50 with a history of cancer; and pain that is consistently worse at rest and at night. Cauda equina syndrome requires emergency spinal surgery to prevent permanent paralysis and incontinence. Neck pain with neurological signs (arm weakness, numbness, difficulty walking) after trauma requires immediate immobilisation and emergency assessment. Risk factors for chronic back pain include poor posture, heavy or repetitive lifting, prolonged sitting, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle. Physical therapy, exercise, and cognitive behavioural therapy are the most effective long-term treatments for chronic back pain.

A mental health crisis occurs when someone s thoughts, feelings, or behaviours put themselves or others at immediate risk. Warning signs of suicidal crisis: talking about wanting to die or kill themselves; expressing feelings of hopelessness, being trapped, or having no reason to live; withdrawing from friends and family; giving away valued possessions; extreme or sudden mood changes; increased use of alcohol or drugs; and talking about being a burden to others. If you believe someone may be at risk of suicide: stay with them and do not leave them alone; listen without judgment; ask directly whether they are thinking about suicide (asking does not plant the idea and can provide relief); remove or secure access to means of suicide such as medicines and firearms if it can be done safely; and contact emergency services or a mental health crisis line (in many countries dial the general emergency number 112, 999, or 911, or a dedicated mental health crisis line). Do not promise to keep suicidal thoughts secret. After a crisis, ensure ongoing connection with mental health services and frequent supportive contact. clinical recommends means restriction as a key population-level suicide prevention strategy. Bereavement from suicide loss also requires compassionate support and access to mental health care.

Early detection of cancer significantly improves survival outcomes. clinical recommends awareness of warning signs that warrant prompt medical evaluation: unexplained persistent weight loss; a new lump or thickening in any part of the body; a persistent cough, hoarseness, or voice change; change in bowel or bladder habits; unusual bleeding or discharge including blood in urine, stool, sputum, or vaginal bleeding between periods or after menopause; a sore or ulcer that does not heal within 3 weeks; difficulty swallowing or persistent indigestion; an obvious change in the size, shape, or colour of a mole or wart; and persistent unexplained pain. These symptoms do not always indicate cancer but should never be dismissed or delayed. Major preventable cancers include: lung cancer (most preventable by stopping smoking); cervical cancer (HPV vaccination and regular cervical screening); liver cancer (hepatitis B vaccination); colorectal cancer (colonoscopy screening from age 50 or 45 in high-risk groups); and breast cancer (mammography screening). Limit alcohol, maintain a healthy weight, exercise regularly, avoid tobacco in all forms, protect skin from UV radiation, and follow recommended vaccination and screening schedules. Seek specialist care promptly; early-stage cancers are often curable with surgery, radiotherapy, and systemic therapy.

Vaccination prevents an estimated 4-5 million deaths per year globally and is one of the most cost-effective public health interventions. clinical-recommended childhood immunisation schedule: at birth give BCG (tuberculosis protection) and hepatitis B birth dose; at 6-14 weeks give DTP (diphtheria, tetanus, whooping cough), Hib (Haemophilus influenzae type b), polio, hepatitis B, and pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; at 9 months give the first measles vaccine (MCV1); at 15-18 months give MCV2 (second measles dose) and a DTP booster. Adults should maintain: tetanus booster every 10 years; annual influenza vaccine especially for the elderly, pregnant women, healthcare workers, young children, and those with chronic conditions; pneumococcal vaccine for adults over 65 or immunocompromised individuals; HPV vaccine for adolescents aged 9-14 years (girls and boys); and shingles (herpes zoster) vaccine for adults over 50. Travellers should receive destination-specific vaccines including yellow fever (required for some countries), typhoid, hepatitis A, and meningococcal vaccines. Missed vaccines can be caught up at any age. Vaccines are rigorously tested for safety and efficacy before licensing; they do not cause the diseases they prevent. Vaccine hesitancy is a major threat to global health; consult a trusted healthcare provider for accurate information.
